text: Dr Michael J RasmussenClinical Director of GynaecologyMercy Hospital for WomenпБљ An UpdateвЧ¶ ClassificationвЧ¶ PathophysiologyпВЦ Early onset Vs late onset diseaseвЧ¶ ScreeningвЧ¶ PreventionпБљ Case ReviewвЧ¶ HypertensionвЧ¶ Essential hypertensionвЧ¶ Preeclampsia, вЧ¶ Fulminant PreeclampsiaпБљ Preeclampsia 5%вЧ¶ Severe 0.5%вЧ¶ Eclampsia 0.05%пВЦ Mortality 1.8%* UKпВЦ World wide mortality highпБљ Responsible for..вЧ¶ 15% maternal deathsвЧ¶ >10% of perinatal deathsвЧ¶ 10% preterm deliveriesвЧ¶ 20% of IOLвЧ¶ 15% of CS1. Pre-eclampsiaвЧ¶ HT >20W with proteinuria, +/- renal or liver upset, neurological problems, haematological disturbance, IUGR2. Gestational HypertensionвЧ¶ HT alone >20W3. Chronic HypertensionвЧ¶ HT preconception or in early pregnancy пВЦessential (90%); secondary (10%)4. Pre-eclampsia superimposed on chronic HTпБљ Early Vs Late diseaseпБљ More than just a spectrum of severityвЧ¶ <34 wksвЧ¶ A primarily fetal disorderвЧ¶ Placental dysfunction, reduced placental volumeпВЦ Incomplete trophoblast spiral artery invasionвЧ¶ IUGR, abnormal dopplers, perinatal hazardвЧ¶ Multiorgan dysfunction and maternal hazardвЧ¶ >34 wksвЧ¶ A primarily maternaldisorderвЧ¶ Secondary to maternal microvascular disease or reflects an underlying genetic disposition вЧ¶ Normal placenta, larger placental volumeпВЦNormal spiral artery behaviourвЧ¶ Normal dopplers, Normal birth weightвЧ¶ More favourable neonatal and maternal outcomesпБљ What NORMALLY happens ....and IS NOT happening in PET?пБљ The Placental BedвЧ¶ Replacement of the endothelium and media smooth muscle cellsвЧ¶ Dilatation to wide вАШnoncontractileвАЩtubesвЧ¶ Loss of vasomotor controlGenetic predispositionPositive FHx: RR=3 for development ofpre-eclampsiaпВЈ No single maternal or fetal gene Genetic predisposition Immunological dysfunction вАШImmune rejectionвАЩ of the genetically foreign fetal allograft. пВЈ Increased PET among primigravid, reduced length of sexual co-habitation, barrier contraception use, sperm and oocyte donation. Antihypertensives?вЧ¶ do NOT reduce the risk of preeclampsia or abruption, or improve fetal outcomeвЧ¶ DO decrease the incidence of severe hypertension.пБљ Vitamin C and E вЧ¶ вАШвА¶does not reduce the risk of pre-eclampsia in nulliparous women, the risk of IUGR or risk of death/ serious outcomes in their infantsвАЩRumbold et al. NEJM 2006вЧ¶ вАШ..does not prevent preeclampsiaвАЩPoston et al, Lancet 2006MgFolic acidFish oil ..no evidence of any benefitвАЩGarlicвЧ¶ вАШвА¶пБљ Low dose aspirinпВЦ selective inhibition of cyclo-oxygenase pathway in platelet production, reducing the synthesis of thromboxane, but not of prostacyclin.вЧ¶ 51 trials; 36,500 womenпВЦ 19% risk reduction in PET: RR 0.81 (0.75-0.88)пВЦ 7% reduction in preterm birthпВЦ 16% reduction in PNMRпБљ вА¶.вАЩreduces the occurrence of PET, preterm birth and fetal and neonatal mortality in women at moderate or high risk of hypertensive disordersвАЩ.пБљ Moderate riskвЧ¶ Mild rise in BP, Abnormal UA flow, Twins, FH of PET, Teenage NullipпБљ High riskвЧ¶ Essential hypertension, Previous severe PET, Diabetes,вЧ¶ 12 trials; 15,206 womenпВЦ 32% risk reduction in PET: RR 0.68 (0.49-0.94)пВЦ Largest reduction, (78%) in studies of high risk women пВЦ The benefit is seen if Calcium intake is low, NOT if it is adequate.пВЦ 1200mg ie 4 servings of milk1.
